bibliographic_data.title: ADAPT: A Pseudo-labeling Approach to Combat Concept Drift in Malware Detection
bibliographic_data.abstract: Machine learning models are commonly used for malware classification; however, they suffer from performance degradation over time due to concept drift. Adapting these models to changing data distributions requires frequent updates, which rely on costly ground truth annotations. While active learning can reduce the annotation burden, leveraging unlabeled data through semi-supervised learning remains a relatively unexplored approach in the context of malware detection. In this research, we introduce ADAPT, a novel pseudo-labeling semi-supervised algorithm for addressing concept drift. Our model-agnostic method can be applied to various machine learning models, including neural networks and tree-based algorithms. We conduct extensive experiments on five diverse malware detection datasets spanning Android, Windows, and PDF domains. The results demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms baseline models and competitive benchmarks.

core_content.novelty_claim: ADAPT is a model-agnostic semi-supervised learning algorithm tailored for concept drift in malware detection, which mitigates catastrophic self-poisoning through drift-aware pseudo-labeling.
core_content.key_methodology: ADAPT uses a drift-aware pseudo-labeling strategy that selectively propagates labels based on class-specific drift behavior. It also incorporates data augmentation and mixup regularization to enhance robustness.
outcomes: Consistently outperforms baseline models and competitive benchmarks across five diverse malware detection datasets.
Effective in an active learning setting, achieving state-of-the-art results on Android malware detection.
core_content.full_text_markdown: ADAPT is a novel pseudo-labeling semi-supervised algorithm designed to combat concept drift in malware detection. Unlike general-purpose methods, ADAPT accounts for the asymmetric nature of drift, which more frequently impacts malware than benign samples. It introduces a drift-aware pseudo-labeling strategy that selectively propagates labels based on class-specific drift behavior. Data augmentation is extended to the concept drift scenario, and mixup regularization is incorporated to improve confidence calibration and mitigate self-poisoning effects. ADAPT consistently outperforms baseline models and competitive benchmarks across five diverse malware detection datasets, including Android, Windows, and PDF domains. It also demonstrates effectiveness in an active learning setting, achieving state-of-the-art results on Android malware detection.
